name: golang-enterprise-patterns
description: Enterprise-level Go architecture patterns including clean architecture,
capabilities: Provide clean architecture guidance for Go applications with proper layer separation
Explain hexagonal architecture with ports and adapters pattern for dependency inversion
Guide Domain-Driven Design implementation including aggregates, value objects, and domain events
Show production-ready project structure with cmd, internal, and pkg organization
Demonstrate proper dependency injection patterns for testable Go code
Provide error handling patterns with custom error types and wrapping
author: Joseph OBrien
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing architectural guidance and Go code examples. Static findings are false positives triggered by documentation patterns. The skill does not contain executable code, scripts, or any mechanism for network calls, command execution, or credential access.
valueStatement: Building maintainable Go applications requires proven architectural patterns. This skill provides clean architecture, hexagonal architecture, and DDD patterns to organize complex codebases for long-term success.
limitations: Does not generate complete application boilerplate code
Does not provide implementation for specific frameworks or databases
Does not include deployment or infrastructure configuration
Does not cover performance optimization or concurrency patterns

useCases.targetUser: Backend engineers designing new Go projects
useCases.title: Design New Enterprise Go Application
useCases.description: Architect a new Go microservice or application using clean architecture principles with proper separation between domain, application, and infrastructure layers.

useCases.targetUser: Senior developers improving existing Go codebases
useCases.title: Refactor Monolithic Go Codebase
useCases.description: Apply hexagonal architecture patterns to extract core business logic from infrastructure dependencies, making the codebase more testable and maintainable.

useCases.title: Implement Domain-Driven Design
useCases.description: Structure complex business domains using DDD patterns including aggregate roots, value objects, and domain events for clear domain boundaries.

promptTemplates.title: New Project Structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me set up a new Go project structure using clean architecture. I need directories for cmd, internal with domain/application/infrastructure/interfaces layers, and pkg for shared libraries. Show me the recommended organization and explain the dependency flow.
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up a new Go project with enterprise patterns

promptTemplates.title: DDD Aggregate Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: I am implementing an order aggregate with DDD patterns. Create an Order aggregate root with proper invariants, value objects for money amounts, and domain events for state changes. Include error handling for invalid state transitions.
promptTemplates.scenario: Implementing DDD aggregates with proper encapsulation

promptTemplates.title: Port and Adapter Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to implement hexagonal architecture for a user management feature. Define driving ports for the API interface, driven ports for storage and notification, and create adapter implementations that depend on abstractions.
promptTemplates.scenario: Applying ports and adapters for dependency inversion

promptTemplates.title: Dependency Injection Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up dependency injection for a Go application. Show manual constructor injection for UserService and OrderService, how to wire dependencies in main.go, and explain when to prefer manual DI over wire or other frameworks.
promptTemplates.scenario: Configuring dependency injection for testable code

bestPractices: Keep the domain layer pure with no framework dependencies - this layer expresses business rules and should be testable without external dependencies
Use small, focused interfaces (interface segregation) - define the minimum contract needed rather than large interfaces
Apply dependency inversion - inner layers define interfaces, outer layers implement them - never let domain depend on infrastructure
antiPatterns: Putting business logic in HTTP handlers - this creates tight coupling between web framework and domain, making testing difficult
Skipping interface definitions - depending directly on concrete implementations prevents mocking and testing
Leaking infrastructure types to domain - importing database or framework types in domain layer breaks architectural boundaries

faq.question: When should I use DDD vs simple CRUD?
faq.answer: Use DDD when you have complex business rules, multiple bounded contexts, or evolving domain logic. Simple CRUD with basic layering works for straightforward applications. DDD adds complexity that pays off when the domain itself is complex.

faq.question: How do I structure a Go microservice with clean architecture?
faq.answer: Place all service code in internal/. cmd/ contains the main.go entry point. Each microservice should have its own domain, application, infrastructure, and interfaces layers. Shared code goes in pkg/ or a separate library.

faq.question: Should I use wire for dependency injection in Go?
faq.answer: Wire is excellent for large applications with many dependencies. For smaller projects, manual constructor injection is simpler and sufficient. Consider manual DI first, then introduce wire when complexity justifies it.

faq.question: How do I test code following clean architecture?
faq.answer: Test domain logic without external dependencies using mock implementations of ports. Application services can be tested with in-memory repository implementations. Integration tests verify adapters work correctly with real databases.

faq.question: What is the difference between application and domain services?
faq.answer: Domain services contain pure business logic that does not fit within an aggregate. Application services coordinate between domain objects and handle use cases, transactions, and cross-cutting concerns like logging.

faq.question: How do I handle errors in clean architecture?
faq.answer: Define domain error types with codes and messages. Wrap infrastructure errors with domain context using fmt.Errorf. Never leak infrastructure errors to upper layers. Use sentinel errors for recoverable conditions.
